title: Dynamical systems on ultra small-world networks
authors: Nirbhay Patil, Ada Altieri, Fabian Aguirre-Lopez
abstract: Despite the knowledge that social, economical, and ecological networks are often of a small-world nature with inter-nodal distance growing even slower than logarithmically with system size, we often assume theoretical systems to be outside of this regime, to make them easier to treat analytically. Here we derive a framework to apply the powerful dynamical mean-field theory on highly heterogeneous networks that is able to account for more of the degree correlations naturally arising from network constraints, known as structural cut-offs. We apply this framework to the well-studied and understood disordered Lotka-Volterra model, and show typically reported observables such as survival rates and stability for these systems on ultra small-world networks. We find much better agreement for these variables for all ranges of exponents for simulated power-law networks as well as empirically sourced networks.
summary: This paper introduces a new theoretical framework to study dynamics on ultra small-world networks by accounting for the degree correlations caused by structural cut-offs. By mapping observed degrees to hidden degrees, the authors successfully predict species survival and stability in highly heterogeneous networks, showing much better agreement with simulations and real-world data than previous models.
main_problem: Standard Heterogeneous Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (HDMFT) fails in 'ultra small-world' networks because it cannot account for the structural cut-offs and degree correlations (disassortativity) that arise in highly heterogeneous, finite-size networks.
main_result: The authors developed a framework using a Soft Configurational Model to map observed degrees to 'hidden degrees,' allowing for accurate prediction of species survival rates and stability in highly heterogeneous networks.
method: The study employs Heterogeneous Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (HDMFT) combined with a maximum entropy ensemble (Soft Configurational Model) and generating functional analysis to derive effective single-species stochastic dynamics.
model_or_system: The system is a Generalized Lotka-Volterra (GLV) model on highly heterogeneous, power-law distributed networks, where species interactions are stochastic and include competition or mutualism.
key_observables: Species survival rates, species abundance distributions, and linear stability of the equilibrium solutions.
important_parameters: Power-law exponent (alpha), mean interaction strength (mu), disorder strength (sigma), reciprocity (gamma), and average connectivity (C).
main_assumptions: The theory assumes a sufficiently large average degree (C) to neglect higher-order terms in 1/C and uses a mapping between hidden and observed degrees to handle structural constraints.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows how structural constraints force negative assortativity; Figure 2 compares survival rate predictions across different joint distribution assumptions; Figure 3 validates the theory against real-world networks; Figure 4 identifies critical system size for stability; Figure 5 demonstrates numerical convergence of the theory.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the failure of existing HDMFT in ultra small-world regimes, proposes a new framework using hidden degrees via the Soft Configurational Model, derives the effective single-species dynamics and stability conditions, and validates the approach using both simulated power-law networks and empirical real-world networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The constraint that any node can have no more than N connections causes highly heteroge- neous networks to have non-trivial correlations, with a non-zero assortativity coefficients r without being constructed specifically to be assortative. Here we show that networks simulated for varying values of the pareto exponent α with fixed N = 4000 and C = 30 are forcibly negatively assorted. The theoretically ex- pected value of the assortativity is 0 if calculated using the statistically independent edge probability function Plin = km/CN, but including the effect of the hidden degrees PSCM(˜k(k), ˜m(m)) we are able to theoretically take into consideration this assortativity.

Figure 2: We compare the predicted survival rate for the various assumptions of the joint distribution func- tion P(k, m) with α = 1.5, µ = −0.1, and σ = 0.3. On the left we plot the survival rate as a function of connectance v = k/N for C = 26, and N = 1162. On the right we show the total survival rate as a function of the system size, while varying C ∝ √

Figure 3: Survival probabilities (left) as a function of connectivity v for µ = −0.1, σ = 0.3 for different networks obtained from [45]. The distributions of the connectances ρ(v) are given on right, and correspond to data from [41–44] in order from (a) to (d). Two of these were asymmetric/directed networks, for which we retained half the edges and made them undirected. The four networks have sizes N(a) = 1133, N(b) = 700, N(c) = 453, and N(d) = 434, with average degree C(a) = 9.6, C(b) = 18.3, C(c) = 9, and C(d) = 70.2. A power-law fit for each would give exponents α(a) = 1.18, α(b) = 0.46, α(c) = 0.66, and α(d) = 0.24.

Figure 4: On the left we plot the value of the system size Nc beyond which any stable system would neces- sarily have degrees of the same order as Nc for a power- law pareto exponent α = 1.5. This highlights the im- portance of degree correlations in different regimes of the phase space for different values of the system size. The dashed lines are the instability predictions to mul- tiple equilibria (white) and unbounded growth (black) without considering degree correlations, while the solid lines are the theoretical predictions including degree correlations. Figure 5: For real networks, the theoretical results converge quite quickly, giving the same results for any Nbins > 30.
